title: Puppy Training: Your First 30 Days – Complete Schedule & Milestones
seoTitle: Puppy Training First 30 Days – Complete Schedule | GetPawsy
seoDescription: Your complete 30-day puppy training plan. Week-by-week milestones for potty training, socialization, basic commands & bite inhibition. Expert guide.
h1Override: Puppy Training: Your First 30 Days – Complete Schedule & Milestones
excerpt: A structured 30-day plan covering potty training, socialization, basic commands, and bite inhibition. Week-by-week milestones with specific daily exercises.
quickAnswer: The first 30 days with a new puppy should focus on four priorities: potty training (days 1–14), socialization (ongoing), basic commands — sit, stay, come (week 2–3), and bite inhibition (week 3–4). Start with short 5-minute training sessions, 3–4 times daily.

bestOverall.name: Puppy Starter Training Kit
bestOverall.reason: Everything you need for the first 30 days in one package.

bestPremium.reason: Harness, leash, clicker, treats, and training guide included.

faq.question: When should I start training my puppy?
faq.answer: Start the day you bring them home. Puppies can begin learning basic cues like 'sit' and their name as early as 8 weeks. The socialization window (8–16 weeks) is the most critical learning period — don't waste it.

faq.question: How long should puppy training sessions be?
faq.answer: Keep sessions to 5 minutes for puppies under 12 weeks, and 10 minutes for 3–6 month old puppies. Short, frequent sessions (3–4 daily) are far more effective than one long session.

faq.question: What commands should I teach first?
faq.answer: Start with: (1) Name recognition, (2) Sit, (3) Come, (4) Stay/Wait. These four form the foundation for all future training. Teach one at a time — don't move on until the current command is reliable.

faq.question: How do I stop my puppy from biting?
faq.answer: Bite inhibition training: when puppy bites, say 'ouch' in a high pitch, stop play for 10 seconds, then resume. This teaches them that biting ends the fun. Redirect to appropriate chew toys. Never use physical punishment.

sections.heading: Week 1: Foundation & Trust Building (Days 1–7)
sections.content:
The first week is about establishing trust and routine — not drilling commands.

**Daily Priorities:**
- Potty breaks every 2 hours (and immediately after eating, napping, playing)
- Crate introduction: feed meals inside, leave door open, positive associations only
- Name recognition: say their name → treat when they look at you. Repeat 20+ times daily
- Handling exercises: gently touch paws, ears, mouth while treating. Builds tolerance for grooming and vet visits

**Socialization (Critical!):**
- Introduce 3–5 new sounds per day (vacuum, doorbell, traffic)
- Let them observe (don't force interaction) new people from a comfortable distance
- Carry them to see different environments if not fully vaccinated

**What NOT to Do:**
- Don't overwhelm them — 2–3 new experiences per day is enough
- Don't punish accidents — they literally can't help it yet
- Don't leave them alone for more than 3 hours

sections.heading: Week 2: First Commands & Potty Progress (Days 8–14)
sections.content:
Your puppy should now recognize their name and follow a basic potty schedule.

**New Training Goals:**
- **Sit**: Lure with treat above nose → bottom hits ground → click/mark → treat. 5 reps, 3 sessions daily
- **Come**: In a hallway, crouch down, say name + 'come', reward enthusiastically when they arrive. Never call for unpleasant things
- **Leave it**: Show treat in closed fist. When puppy stops sniffing → mark + treat from OTHER hand. This saves lives later

**Potty Training Progress Check:**
- By day 14, you should see 70–80% success rate
- If below 50%, tighten the schedule (go out every 90 minutes) and reduce unsupervised free time

**Leash Introduction:**
- Let puppy drag a lightweight leash indoors while supervised
- Practice following you with treats — no pulling corrections yet
- First outdoor walks: short (5 minutes per month of age), let them sniff

See our [leash training collection](/collections/dog-leash-control) for puppy-appropriate harnesses and leads.

sections.heading: Week 3: Building Reliability & Bite Inhibition (Days 15–21)
sections.content:
Focus shifts to making learned behaviors reliable and addressing puppy biting.

**Training Refinement:**
- **Sit**: Add duration — ask for sit, count to 3, then treat. Gradually increase to 10 seconds
- **Stay**: With puppy sitting, take one step back → return → treat. Build to 5 steps over the week
- **Down**: From sit, lure treat to floor between front paws. Shape gradually
- Practice all commands in different rooms — puppies don't generalize well

**Bite Inhibition Protocol:**
- When puppy bites: yelp 'ouch!' → stop play for 10 seconds → resume
- Redirect all mouthing to appropriate toys immediately
- If biting escalates during play, that play session ends completely
- Provide frozen washcloths and rubber chew toys for teething discomfort

**Socialization Expansion:**
- Meet 5 new people this week (different ages, appearances)
- Hear 5 new sounds (construction, thunderstorms via recordings)
- Walk on 3 different surfaces (grass, concrete, gravel)

sections.heading: Week 4: Independence & Real-World Practice (Days 22–30)
sections.content:
Your puppy should now have reliable basics in familiar settings. Time to level up.

**Advanced Practice:**
- Practice commands outdoors with distractions (start easy — quiet park, not dog park)
- Introduce 'wait' at doors — puppy must sit before going through any doorway
- Begin recall practice on a [long training line](/collections/dog-training-accessories) (15 ft) in a fenced area

**Potty Training Milestone:**
- Target: 90%+ success rate by day 30
- If achieved, cautiously expand supervised free-roam time
- If not, no shame — some breeds take 6–8 weeks. Stay consistent

**Crate Training Progress:**
- Puppy should tolerate crate for 2–3 hours during the day
- Nighttime: most puppies can sleep 6–8 hours by week 4
- If still crying at night, the crate may be too isolated — move it closer to your bedroom

**30-Day Assessment:**
Your puppy should now: recognize their name, sit on cue, come when called in low-distraction environments, tolerate handling, and follow a potty schedule. If any area is weak, spend week 5 reinforcing it before adding new skills.

sections.heading: Essential Tools for the First 30 Days
sections.content:
Having the right tools from day one accelerates every aspect of training:

- **Adjustable puppy harness** — Step-in design that grows with your puppy. [Browse options →](/collections/dog-leash-control)
- **6-foot fixed leash** — Never a retractable for puppies. Fixed leash teaches proper walking
- **Clicker or marker word** — Precision timing for marking correct behaviors
- **Treat pouch** — Hands-free access to rewards during training sessions. [See accessories →](/collections/dog-training-accessories)
- **Potty pads** (for apartment training) — [Browse potty gear →](/collections/dog-potty-training)
- **Enzymatic cleaner** — For accident cleanup that eliminates scent markers
- **Crate** — Appropriately sized (room to stand, turn, lie down — no bigger)

All these tools are curated in our [Puppy Training Essentials collection](/collections/puppy-training-essentials).
